title: Galaxy Unpacked Confirmed, Apple Sues OpenAI, and a Gmail Fix You'll Actually Use
intro: Samsung locks in its July 22 Unpacked date, Apple takes OpenAI to court over trade secrets, and a Gmail setting most people never touch.

posts.hook: Samsung Unpacked confirmed for July 22 in London
posts.body: Samsung has officially confirmed Galaxy Unpacked for July 22 in London. Expected, though still rumor until stage time: the Galaxy Z Fold8 and Fold8 Ultra, the Z Flip8, a couple of new Galaxy Watches, and possibly Samsung's first smart glasses. Pre-orders are expected to open the same day, with sales starting around August 7. Foldables are clearly the story of this cycle — the real question is whether the Fold8 Ultra's price jump is worth it over a regular Fold8.
posts.problem: Ahead of any official confirmation, foldable rumors had been swirling for months with no fixed date to anchor them, making it hard to know what was real planning versus wishful leaking.
posts.breakthrough: Samsung has officially locked in Galaxy Unpacked for July 22 in London, with the Z Fold8 and Fold8 Ultra, Z Flip8, new Galaxy Watches, and possibly Samsung's first smart glasses all expected on stage. Pre-orders are expected to open the same day, with sales starting around August 7.
posts.catch: Everything except the date itself is still rumor until Samsung actually takes the stage — device names, pricing, and specs could all shift between now and July 22.
posts.forYou: The real decision point isn't whether to get excited about the event — it's whether the Fold8 Ultra's price premium over a regular Fold8 will actually be worth it once the specs are official, so hold off on preorder decisions until pricing is confirmed.

posts.hook: Apple sues OpenAI over alleged trade secret theft
posts.body: Apple has officially sued OpenAI, filing suit on July 10 in a California federal court. The claim: OpenAI stole Apple trade secrets to build its own hardware, with theft occurring "at every level," from technical staff up to OpenAI's Chief Hardware Officer. Two allegations stand out — OpenAI's hardware chief, an ex-Apple VP, allegedly told job candidates to bring actual Apple parts to interviews for "show and tell," and a former Apple engineer allegedly kept his company laptop after joining OpenAI and pulled confidential documents off it. OpenAI says it has "no interest in other companies' trade secrets." Two years ago these companies were partners — now it's a lawsuit.
posts.problem: Apple and OpenAI were close partners two years ago, with ChatGPT built directly into iOS — a friendly relationship that masked a deeper, unresolved tension over AI talent and hardware ambitions.
posts.breakthrough: Apple officially sued OpenAI on July 10 in California federal court, alleging trade-secret theft 'at every level' — including claims that OpenAI's hardware chief, an ex-Apple VP, told job candidates to bring actual Apple parts to interviews, and that a former Apple engineer kept his company laptop after joining OpenAI and pulled confidential documents off it.
posts.catch: OpenAI has publicly denied any interest in other companies' trade secrets, and specific, dramatic allegations like these still have to hold up under discovery and cross-examination — a compelling complaint isn't the same as a proven case.
posts.forYou: This is the clearest sign yet that the AI partnership era between platform owners and model labs is ending — expect more of Apple's AI hardware roadmap to be built defensively, in courtrooms as much as in labs.

posts.hook: Your Gmail Undo Send window is shorter than it needs to be
posts.body: Most people don't know Gmail's Undo Send window is adjustable. The default is 5 seconds — barely enough time to blink. Head to Settings, See all settings, General, Undo Send, and bump the send cancellation period to 30 seconds. That's a full 30 seconds to catch the wrong attachment, an accidental reply-all, or a typo before it's too late.
posts.problem: Gmail's default Undo Send window is just 5 seconds — barely enough time to notice a mistake, let alone act on it, before an email is gone for good.
posts.breakthrough: That window is fully adjustable: head to Settings, See all settings, General, Undo Send, and you can extend the cancellation period to a full 30 seconds.
posts.catch: It's not turned on by default and easy to miss since it's buried a few menus deep — most people never go looking for a setting they don't know exists.
posts.forYou: Bump it to 30 seconds today — it's the easiest fix for the wrong attachment, an accidental reply-all, or a typo you'd otherwise regret the moment you hit send.

posts.hook: Turn a pile of receipts into an expense sheet in seconds
posts.body: Here's an AI workflow that actually saves time: next time you have a pile of receipts to expense, snap photos of all of them, upload to Gemini, ChatGPT, or Claude, and prompt it to read the receipts and return a table with vendor, date, amount, and category for each. You get a clean, copy-paste-ready expense sheet in seconds instead of 20 minutes of manual entry.
posts.problem: Turning a pile of paper receipts into a clean expense report is one of the most tedious recurring tasks in any job that involves travel or reimbursement — 20 minutes of manual typing for something a computer should handle.
posts.breakthrough: Snap photos of every receipt, upload them to Gemini, ChatGPT, or Claude, and ask it to read them and return a table with vendor, date, amount, and category for each one — a clean, copy-paste-ready expense sheet in seconds.
posts.catch: Accuracy depends on photo quality and receipt condition — faded thermal paper or awkward angles can trip up the read, so it's worth a quick scan of the output before you submit it.
posts.forYou: Next expense report, skip the manual entry entirely — this workflow turns a 20-minute chore into a 30-second copy-paste job.

posts.hook: Book-style fold or wallet-style fold — which wins?
posts.body: Samsung has spent eight generations refining the book-style fold that opens like a mini tablet, with the Fold8 landing July 22. Apple's rumored foldable is expected to take a different approach — a more compact, wallet-style vertical fold closer to a flip phone that opens into a bigger screen. Two very different bets on what people actually want from a foldable phone.
